Now, I would like to quickly explain why I have marked Gladstone Investment (GAIN) and Blue Owl Technology Finance Corp. (OTF) in blue. Optically, their adjusted base dividend coverage levels look extremely unsustainable.

However, here is the thing.

When it comes to GAIN we have to appreciate that it has entered into a significant growth mode, expanding its portfolio at a rapid pace. There always is a lag between when the debt is assumed (reflected on the books) and when the fresh capital gets deployed and starts to generate accretive NII. This is what we are seeing now. Plus, GAIN has, arguably, one of the strongest equity positions in the game (43% of its book is linked to equity holdings). Mainly because of the high-quality equity book, GAIN in Q4 was able to record a NAV per share growth of 10% compared to the prior quarter. In other words, GAIN has the right tailwinds to its Q4 NII per share and an attractive equity book that should help preserve the dividend going forward.

Speaking of OTF, it boils down to the same essence as for GAIN. For example, in Q3 OTF had a debt-to-equity of ~0.60x; now it is close to 0.80x. This is a massive growth that is likely to continue until optimal capital structure is reached (at around 1.15x). On top of that, OTF has a meaningful equity book that, among other things, includes SpaceX, which could be successfully monetized. Plus, the management has sent a clear signal that it remains committed to the current base dividend.

Key takeaways

As you could see in the table above, most BDCs have very tight base dividend coverage levels, which get worse if we adjust them for non-cash income (PIK).

The situation gets even more challenging if we consider the balance sheet capacities, which for quite many BDCs are exhausted or, worse, in a territory that would require deleveraging efforts (obviously negative to NII per share).

Some BDCs have equity portfolios that might bring in sizeable gains that could be used for deleveraging or portfolio growth. However, I would not assign too high a probability of that happening this year because of the elevated uncertainty that we have across the board (bad for M&A).

Issuing fresh equity is a rare luxury in the BDC space now and is only available for those BDCs that have high-quality assets and a decent margin of safety (which already imply sustainable payouts).

To make the matter worse, I even would suggest taking the Q4 NII per share generation levels with a grain of salt (and thus the adjusted base dividend coverage levels that I've reflected above). Here are the main reasons:

  • Many BDCs have unfavorable (below market rate) debt refinancings on the horizon.
  • The realized base rate cuts that took place in Q4 have some lagged effects that in Q4 2025 earnings figures have not yet percolated through the system (i.e., there will be lower SOFR driven headwinds in Q1 2026).
  • And this is the most important aspect - the BDC non-accruals are currently very low, and it should not come as a surprise that we see an uptick here quite soon (think of SaaS risks, inflationary dynamics, uncertainty and consequences from the war in Iran etc.). Rising non-accruals can quickly destroy all margin of safety and lead to sizeable NAV (and dividend) reductions. For example, before Oaktree Specialty Lending Corporation (OCSL), TriplePoint Venture Growth (TPVG) and FS KKR Capital Corp. (FSK) slashed their dividend due to surging non-accruals, their base dividend coverage levels stood (approximately) between 115% and 120%.

So, I would not be surprised seeing Ares Capital (ARCC) and SLR Investment (SLRC) (two BDCs that I don't expect cutting this year) revisit their dividend downwards if they saw a 1.5%+ increase in non-accruals. The margin of safety is not that strong for them.

Final comments

I would suggest investors ignore or at least deemphasize BDCs that I have not marked in blue if experiencing a dividend cut is a clear no-go.

However, I would also like to underscore that even after dividend cuts, most post-cut yields should remain at attractive levels, mainly because of the system-wide discounts.

The average base dividend cut that has been realized so far by quite many BDCs is around 25%. Let's say that Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL) which is a high-quality BDC cuts its dividend by 20%, we would still be left with a nice 10.3% yielding income machine.

In a nutshell, I would not make my decisions on which BDCs to invest in or not just based on the near-term base dividend prospects. I would use this information as a solid starting point to contextualize the case with, say, P/NAV multiple and loan book quality.

data.title: Lockheed Martin: Strong Franchise, Limited Upside At Current Valuation
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT) has an established position as a quality defense franchise that will continue to be relevant into the future; however, I see limited short-term upside given today's valuation. The leadership position that Lockheed maintains in next-generation technologies, including hypersonics, will continue to create a barrier to entry and reinforce Lockheed's competitive advantage in the future. Unfortunately, both of these areas have yet to reach maturity and are currently in early stages of development, so they are unlikely to contribute much margin or capital efficiency until later in the decade.

In addition to the new next-generation capability space, Lockheed's traditional platform-based businesses also represent a stable base of revenue; however, they provide very low organic growth rates in the low single-digits. This makes Lockheed's growth profile structurally inconsistent with its current valuation of ~29x earnings. Although this valuation is not overly rich historically, it does leave very little room for multiple expansion.

There is additional risk associated with the execution of the company's strategy. The integration of hypersonic technology on the Zumwalt destroyer adds some level of uncertainty related to technology and scheduling. Even with a defense supercycle, there are other macro factors that can negatively impact the upside potential of Lockheed's stock, including a shift in investor preference towards new emerging defense companies that leverage AI. After the near 30% rally, Lockheed now meets many of the characteristics of a "capital preservation" type of investment versus a source of growth. Since I believe much of the growth expectations have been priced in, I'm rating the stock a Hold.

Hypersonic Win

Lockheed’s $1.35 billion contract to provide the common peripheral system (CPS) for the U.S. Navy is a positive development. The company will be the prime contractor responsible for delivering the Navy’s first at-sea hypersonic strike capability. In fact, the USS Zumwalt will no longer have its forward gun mount; instead, it will have launch tubes installed in place of the gun mount. This makes Lockheed the primary integrator for this project. Live fire testing is expected to begin in 2026 followed by initial operational capability.

In total, the contract, when all options are included, will increase the value to just under $2 billion. Additionally, Lockheed will serve as the lead contractor for the Long-Range Hypersonic Weapon (LRHW) program for the U.S. Army.

While there is a significant amount of strategic importance associated with Lockheed being the lead contractor for this country’s first operational at-sea hypersonic platform, in terms of near-term revenue potential, this program represents a very small fraction of Lockheed's overall annual revenue. Last year, that annual revenue was $75 billion. Therefore, revenue from this new platform represents approximately 2% of Lockheed's annual revenue, which is not too impactful on additional growth or revenue opportunities for investors. Furthermore, this program is currently in the early stages of development and integration, which represents the most capital intensive, lowest margin phase of a program’s life cycle. It may take a few years before solid production and sustainment dollars flow into Lockheed from this program. With a rapid rise YTD, the market appears to have priced in the majority of expectations about this win and the overall defense supercycle.

Domestic F-35 Ceiling

The F-35 continues to serve as the primary foundation of Lockheed Martin's product line, accounting for nearly 27% of the company's total consolidated revenues in 2025. The program generates a tremendous amount of consistent, predictable revenue, which represents one of the key advantages of being such a large player in a single market. However, because there is a limit to how many F-35s can be made domestically under the allocated budget, especially when considering the Air Force and Navy have transitioned their focus to NGAD, the rate at which new F-35 airframes will be ordered by the U.S. military will no longer represent the principal source of growth that it once represented for Lockheed. Therefore, going forward, Lockheed will need to depend on both foreign sales and long-term sustainment efforts. These represent stable sources of revenue; however, they are unlikely to provide the same type of opportunity for upside potential that would support a further multiple expansion.

While Lockheed had success clearing its parked-jet inventory and produced a program-high number of aircraft in FY2025 (191), a majority of those aircraft were equipped with a partial TR-3 software package. Consequently, those aircraft are unable to perform actual combat operations. In an early 2026 report to Congress, the DOT&E reported that TR-3 software development has stalled and that no additional combat-ready versions of TR-3 have been developed since last year due to issues related to stability.

This status presents an impending financial and operational challenge. Since TR-3 is considered to be the fundamental hardware and software backbone upon which all of the Block 4 upgrades are based, the delayed delivery of TR-3 software will delay the complete upgrade capability of all F-35s until 2031, and increase the overall cost of the entire program by an estimated $6 billion. Even though the F-35 numbers look strong, investors may overlook the costs associated with retrofitting the jets for full mission readiness. This adds on more long-term execution risk, where the future software upgrades could weigh on margins even after the current defense supercycle peaks.

Rotation Risk

With a defense supercycle, institutional investors have increased allocations to the defense sector. With this supercycle, investors are looking for the next big winners; these tend to be the most attractive stocks with the highest growth potential. Therefore, as investors become more interested in high-growth defense stocks, they begin to favor AI-enabled defense technologies (e.g., Palantir's (PLTR) battlefield intelligence, autonomous systems from Anduril, etc.), there will be significant amounts of money flowing into growth stories that legacy companies (like Lockheed) might struggle to deliver.

It does not make Lockheed an outdated investment; rather, it changes what type of investment Lockheed is. This now changes the type of investor who will chase an investment like Lockheed, especially after this recent rally, where buyers will be those institutional investors who buy shares for their stable returns on equity, dividends, and defense exposure. However, institutional portfolio managers who have already seen names like Palantir increase by over 200% in less than two years and whose portfolios need to outperform the market may find it difficult to allocate additional dollars to traditional defense. With legacy defense companies like Lockheed trading at 29x trailing P/E, it may be hard to justify an investment.

This dynamic has existed for some time; however, it becomes more pronounced when Lockheed shares trade close to the upper end of their historic valuation multiples. In my view, Lockheed shares are neither sufficiently inexpensive to attract value buyers nor do they offer sufficient growth potential to attract growth buyers. This is especially true after this year's rally, where growth buyers have had strong returns. However, this growth seems now priced in, leading me to categorize it as a hold given the current story.

Execution Risk: Zumwalt's Track Record

The USS Zumwalt-class destroyers have a documented history of mission changes and cost overruns, which eventually led them to cut planned production by 91%. This kind of history should add a higher level of execution risk to this hypersonic integration program compared to other programs. The Zumwalt-class was originally designed as land-attack vessels with advanced gun systems that fired GPS-guided shells, costing $800,000 each, which was so expensive the program was quietly canceled. Now, the class is being repurposed for exactly the hypersonic strike capabilities Lockheed is supposed to deliver.

The physical integration work for the hypersonic missile tubes was completed by 2024. But live fire testing and IOC still remain to be done. Any technical delay in hypersonic integration, delay in the naval test schedule, or issue with the CPS missile itself could delay revenue recognition into future years and create margin pressure in the corresponding segment. Besides the minimal revenue, any delays or overruns, which the program has had historically, could impact margins and expected contribution to overall revenue.

Valuation

Lockheed is currently trading at approximately ~$623 with its trailing P/E multiple at ~29x. This multiple has only been growing, due to the defense supercycle and optimism on new programs. While these have merit, I believe that optimism to be priced in at current levels with the multiple at 5-year highs.

Although defense spending is at an all-time high, not all of that money will go to legacy companies like Lockheed. So far, the company has done well to extend the lifetime of its F-35 program, but future operations are still uncertain and carry significant execution risk relative to legacy projects. Lockheed will benefit, but so will up-and-comers like Palantir and Anduril, which have seen their valuations soar in recent years. At the current valuation, the rewards and risks seem fairly priced in, which leads me to rate Lockheed a Hold.

Risks

The most likely positive scenario for Lockheed would result from an earlier-than-expected TR-3 resolution. This would allow for the withholding of previously unpaid amounts by the Department of Defense and cause an acceleration in cash flows likely beyond what is currently estimated.

Global conflicts escalating to a higher level of intensity, including those with adversarial nations possessing hypersonic weapons capabilities, could lead to increased U.S. military procurement requirements and potentially advance the timeline of revenue growth.

The largest increase in funding for defense spending at the federal government level (Congress passing legislation appropriating significantly more than projected) would positively impact all defense contractors, especially a large player like Lockheed. In my view, with the stock trading at elevated multiples, I believe the majority of this is priced in.

Conclusion

Lockheed Martin continues to be one of the largest and most consistent players in the global defense market and holds a dominant position in both legacy and emerging technologies. The company's role in developing hypersonic weapons and their sustained dominance in the development of the F-35 fighter aircraft provide Lockheed with a significant place in future U.S. defense expenditures for many years to come.

However, when evaluating Lockheed from a long-term investment perspective, the company does not offer the most compelling investment narrative. As Lockheed develops its new generation of products and services, it is estimated to take at least several years before they begin to impact Lockheed's reported earnings. Lockheed is now trading at a premium multiple relative to its history, which prices in tailwinds from a defense supercycle. In addition, Lockheed's relatively low organic growth rate combined with increased competition for capital within the defense industry limits the likelihood of continued multiple expansion.

Therefore, given Lockheed's elevated valuation and current situation, the stock should be evaluated as a stable and defensive investment option rather than a high conviction buy. In my view, LMT stock is at a fair valuation where I see limited near-term upside, which is why I'm rating Lockheed a Hold.

data.title: Zai Lab: Xacduro And ZL-1310 Drive Growth Narrative
data.description:
Zai Lab Limited (ZLAB) is one of my favorites in China's pharmaceutical industry. It has a rich pipeline of product candidates focused on the treatment of cancer and immune-mediated inflammatory diseases.

But the vast majority of its revenue currently comes from agreements with Big Pharma players to commercialize their medications, such as argenx's Vyvgart (ARGX), GSK's Zejula (GSK), and Bristol-Myers Squibb's (BMY) Cobenfy/Augtyro, in Greater China.

In this article, I will present 3 factors explaining why I'm initiating coverage of it with a Buy rating.

Current valuation of Zai Lab

Its revenue rose 17% year-over-year to $127.6 million in Q4. It also beat the Wall Street consensus estimate by $4.9 million.

I believe this was primarily due to a jump in net revenue of Zejula to about $56 million despite tightening competition in the recurrent ovarian cancer market from AstraZeneca's Lynparza (AZN) and BeOne's pamiparib (ONC).

Since I've discussed this PARP inhibitor in more detail previously in articles about GSK, such as "GSK: Depemokimab and Jemperli Lead Next Wave," I'll now move on to an equally important drug in Zai Lab's portfolio, Xacduro [sulbactam/durlobactam].

But first, I will remind you that Xacduro was approved by China's NMPA on May 20, 2024, for the treatment of pneumonia caused by bacteria called Acinetobacter.

Sadly, Acinetobacter is difficult to treat due to its high levels of antibiotic resistance. And so before the launch of sulbactam/durlobactam, there was an urgent medical need for new antibiotics.

But why do I think Xacduro can be called a "savior"?

In the Phase 3 ATTACK study [NCT03894046], it reduced 28-day all-cause mortality by 13.2% compared to the "gold standard," colistin [P<0.001].

And this clinical data is reflecting positively on the sales of Xacduro.

So, it generated about $10.7 million in revenue for Zai Lab in Q4, up 66.5% QoQ and beating my "base case" scenario by $1.5 million.

Zai Lab's pipeline of experimental drugs

Overall, it has a rich pipeline of molecules at various stages of clinical development.

But among these, I believe ZL-1310 [zocilurtatug pelitecan] has the greatest commercial potential.

Zocilurtatug pelitecan is an antibody-drug conjugate.

That is, one part of its molecule consists of an anti-DLL3 monoclonal antibody, which is connected via a tripeptide-based linker to an anticancer agent, a topoisomerase 1 inhibitor (marked in red for clarity).

This experimental drug interested me for 3 reasons.

It is being developed against a very common and aggressive type of cancer called SCLC [small cell lung cancer], which accounts for about 15% of all lung cancer diagnoses.

To my great regret, WHO's Cancer Tomorrow doesn't provide numbers only for extensive-stage SCLC, but even without that, it's clear that the TAM is big, as about 4.62 million cases of trachea, bronchus and lung [2.95 million men and 1.67 million women] will be diagnosed in 2050, up 86.2% rise from 2022.

Secondly, on April 1st, Zai Lab announced a partnership with Amgen (AMGN). Under the deal, Amgen will fund the Phase 1b trial to evaluate the efficacy of ZL-1310 with its DLL3/CD3 bispecific antibody, Imdelltra.

In my view, since these drugs have complementary mechanisms, this could potentially help overcome resistance pathways, increasing OS and progression-free survival.

And the last reason, of course, is the results of the Phase 1study [NCT06179069].

In it, pre-treated patients with ES-SCLC who received 1.6 mg/kg ZL-1310 as 2L therapy achieved the best ORR [overall response rate] of 68%, which is higher than blockbusters such as AstraZeneca's Imfinzi (AZN) or Bristol-Myers Squibb's Opdivo.

Long-Term prospects of Zai Lab

I expect its revenue to continue to grow until 2030, mainly due to increased sales of Nuzyra, Xacduro, and Zejula.

By the way, net revenue of the "gem" called Nuzyra for the treatment of ABSSSI and community-acquired bacterial pneumonia was $16 million in Q4, up 45.5% YoY and 3.7% quarter-on-quarter.

Also, in my DCF model, I factored potential approvals of its product candidates, including povetacicept [IgA nephropathy], zocilurtatug pelitecan [ES-SCLC], and elegrobart [active thyroid eye disease] by the NMPA.

And in forecasting Zai Lab's revenue and EBIT, I took into account the subdued demand for Vyvgart/Vyvgart Hytrulo, as well as tightening competition in the non-small cell lung cancer market, especially from Eisai/Nuvation's taletrectinib (ESALF) (NUVB).

Thus, in my estimation, its total revenue will be $1.56 billion in 2030, which firstly implies its annual growth from 9.7% to 47.9%, and secondly, it is in line with the expectations of 4 analysts [$1.22 billion to $1.7 billion].

Accordingly, this will lead to a drop in Zai Lab's price-to-sales ratio from 4.86x over the last 12 months to below 1.5x.

What are my and Wall Street analysts' expectations for Zai Lab's earnings over the next 5 years?

And here, things are worse relative to its revenue. So, the consensus estimate for Q1 is -0.48 cents [from -0.58 cents to -0.41 cents], representing a year-over-year drop of 6.8%.

However, in my view, not everything is so bad if I look at the broader picture.

So, given Zai Lab's revenue growth rate I mentioned earlier and my assumption that Tivdak will be approved by the NMPA in mid-May 2026 for the treatment of cervical cancer, this will lead to triple/double-digit growth in its non-GAAP EPS [from 36.3% to 660%] between 2027 and 2030.

Also, according to my model, this financial metric is above the median of the Wall Street analysts' forecast range [$0.4-$2.1]. Because I have more optimistic expectations regarding the sales of "duo," Nuzyra and Xacduro.

As a result, ZLAB's earnings per share will be $1.52 in 2030, which corresponds to a price-to-earnings ratio of 13.4x, 22.1% lower than the sector median.

Risks

Now, it's time for my table with risks.

They include, among others, potential competition from Merck's Keytruda (MRK) and Jazz Pharmaceuticals' Zepzelca (JAZZ), and RFK Jr./Trump's information pressure on the sector.

Takeaway

2026 will be a year of multiple key data readouts and catalysts for Zai Lab.

So, I'm especially waiting for the launch of Cobenfy in China in Q2 for the treatment of schizophrenia, which, by the way, affects about 5.3 million people.

And in addition to the investment theses I highlighted earlier in the article, another one is that the average price target for Zai Lab is $38.47.

That is, this implies an upside of about 88.5% from the current ZLAB stock price.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Stock Market Rally: Buy Or Fade It?
data.description:
Last week, the stock market rally was one of the best performances in nearly a year. The S&P 500 surged 3.4%, the Nasdaq climbed 4.4%, and the bulls declared the correction over. As I have stated before, having watched markets for more than 35 years, I have come to recognize the difference between a relief rally and the end of a corrective cycle. So far, this remains a relief rally until overhead resistance is broken through and successfully retested. The question that matters now is whether the stock market rally has the institutional support to break through those resistance levels or whether Monday’s open will reveal the reversal was already finished before most investors realized it started.

The answer, based on every technical and macro lens I use, points heavily toward the latter.

Let’s Review The Tape

The S&P 500 closed at 6,582, bouncing roughly 4.5% off the late-March lows near 6,300. That sounds constructive until you examine what those price levels represent. The index pushed directly into its 200-day moving average near 6,642 and remains comfortably below the 50-day moving average near 6,789. The 20-DMA trends around 6,607. Together, those three levels form an overhead cluster of resistance that has historically acted as a gravitational ceiling for stocks in corrective environments. Furthermore, the volume profile we analyzed on Wednesday confirmed the same. Many investors are trapped at those prices, holding positions at a loss and waiting for any rally to exit at break-even, which adds to the risk of a relief rally failure.

Then came Thursday’s escalation, following President Trump’s prime-time address, which offered no clear path toward ending the conflict or reopening the Strait of Hormuz. Unsurprisingly, oil responded immediately. WTI crude surged to $111.54 per barrel on settlement Thursday, the highest close since June 2022. Equity futures reversed what the bulls had built over two sessions, trading sharply lower Thursday morning before recovering to a small gain by the close. The only thing that likely helped the market end on a good note was that markets were closed for Good Friday. With the jobs report posting a gain of 178,000 on Friday, the market would likely have sold off on the expectation that the Federal Reserve is now fully trapped in terms of monetary policy.

With that said, we need to focus on what the market is telling us, which is where the MFBR index comes in.

Why You Should Listen To The MFBR Index

Our Money Flow Breadth Ratio, a rules-based institutional flow indicator we’ve backtested across 1,351 weekly observations from 2000 through 2025, currently stands at 35% and is declining.

That places it squarely in the single worst sub-range within SELL territory and historically suggests a higher degree of caution. The MFBR works by tracking weekly net dollar money flow in the S&P 500 index, counting the number of the last 20 weeks that showed positive institutional buying, and converting that count into a ratio. Readings above 60% signal sustained buying pressure, whereas readings below 40% signal sustained selling pressure. Notably, the model goes a step further and also incorporates the direction of the MFBR, whether it is rising or falling. This is because a 25-year backtest from 2000 to 2025 demonstrated that trajectory matters as much as level, particularly in transition zones.

At 35% and declining, the data is unambiguous about what history suggests will happen next. Across 73 observations in the 35–40% zone, average forward returns were negative at one month (−1.1%), three months (−1.2%), and six months (−0.3%). The six-month win rate was just 34.7%, meaning the market was lower six months later nearly two out of every three times. The twelve-month win rate recovered to 58.6%, but that is still well below the all-period baseline of 75.7%. The backtest’s range of outcomes, from a best-case of +66.6% to a worst-case of −29.1% over 12 months, underscores why maximum defensiveness is the appropriate posture right now.

Bottom line: Until the MFBR stabilizes and recovers sustainably above 50%, patience over aggression and capital preservation over return-chasing are the rules. The one exception: if the MFBR drops below 30%, historical data shows a genuine contrarian buy signal with a 100% win rate at twelve months. We are not there yet.

That is just the technical data; other factors are at play that may also have an impact.

Other Risks: Oil, Interest Rates & The Fed’s Dilemma

The macro backdrop has rarely been this unfavorable for equities simultaneously across so many dimensions. Oil is the primary driver right now, with Brent crude oil spot price rising sharply following the onset of military action in the Middle East. With petroleum shipments through the Strait of Hormuz being disrupted and significant Middle East production shut in, recession risks are rising. The longer the situation persists, the greater the risk that the oil impulse translates into broader inflationary pressures. (As shown, there is a decent correlation between oil prices and CPI.)

That puts the Federal Reserve in a genuinely impossible position. It is also one that should concern every equity investor far more than the day-to-day volatility. Markets are now pricing zero rate cuts for the remainder of 2026. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) closed at 4.31%, while the 30-year yield (US30Y) was 4.88%. Fed Chair Powell has explicitly acknowledged the tension.

“Inflation remains above target, the labor market is softening, and the conflict has introduced significant uncertainty into every forecasting model the FOMC relies on.”

This is the risk where energy-driven inflation squeezes real purchasing power while employment deteriorates. The Fed cannot cut without inflaming inflation expectations, and it cannot raise rates without breaking an already-fragile labor market. The result is paralysis at exactly the moment markets need a clear policy anchor.

The jobs report adds another layer of complexity. March’s nonfarm payrolls print came in at +178,000. That was much stronger than the 60,000 estimates, and the unemployment rate dropped to 4.3%. With the market closed on Friday, the reaction will be delayed until Monday’s opening.

Furthermore, last week’s stock market rally was not broad, which matters far more than the headline index gain suggests. As of April 2, just 27.6% of S&P 500 constituents were trading above their 50-day moving averages. That is up from below 20% at the March low but down ~70% during the broad-based advance earlier this year. The percentage of S&P 500 stocks above their 200-day moving average sits below 50%. These types of readings are more akin to corrections and bear markets than bull markets.

There are positives to consider. Relative strength (RSI) on the S&P 500 recovered from deeply oversold levels near 30 in late March to approximately 45. That is encouraging from a mean-reversion perspective; however, it is still below the neutral 50 level. The VIX is holding near 28, confirming that institutional hedging demand remains elevated. When professionals are still buying protection at these levels, they are telling you something about their conviction in the rally. Momentum, as measured by the MACD indicator, remains in negative territory but did trigger a short-term buy signal. However, these momentum conditions are consistent with what we’ve seen historically in corrective environments. Short-covering bounces tend to fade back into the primary downtrend.

April seasonality deserves acknowledgment. Historically, April has been one of the stronger months for equities. But that historical average includes far calmer geopolitical and macroeconomic backdrops than today’s. A seasonal tailwind is a weak force compared to oil at $111, a frozen Fed, and a still weak labor market that hasn’t fully felt the oil price spike. Furthermore, the MFBR data also points to sustained institutional selling pressure.

So, while there are certainly reasons to be bullish, there is risk in that view.

Where The Bullish Case Potentially Fails

I take the bull case seriously because good analysis requires it, and there are genuine counterarguments worth acknowledging.

  • First, the MFBR’s own 25-year backtest shows that when the ratio drops below 30%, a true capitulation washout has occurred. The subsequent returns flip dramatically positive: +5.1% at one month, +7.8% at six months, and a 100% win rate at twelve months. We’re not there yet, but that threshold isn’t far from the current 35% reading.
  • Second, the volume profile below 6,300 thins out considerably. The lack of sellers could allow prices to squeeze back toward the resistance without much friction if buyers step in.
  • Third, the first-quarter earnings season begins next week. FactSet currently projects roughly 13% S&P 500 earnings growth. If that number is confirmed by actual results, it could provide the fundamental catalyst bulls need.

The problem is that none of those factors changes the immediate risk-reward math. The 200-DMA remains resistant on a closing basis, despite last week’s stock market rally. Breadth has not recovered. Institutional money flow is in sell territory and falling. Oil is near a multi-year high with no clear resolution in sight. And the biggest monthly economic data release of April dropped on a closed market, deferring the full reaction to Monday. At the current MFBR reading, the historical six-month win rate is 34.7%. That reading indicates that selling near this resistance remains the correct trade in roughly 2 out of 3 comparable setups.

For now, the burden of proof is on the bulls, and last week’s rally has not yet met it.

As such, following last week’s stock market rally, here are seven actions to consider when trading begins on Monday.

The summer sets up as one of the more difficult stretches for equities in recent memory. Oil above $100 for any sustained period has historically pressured both consumer spending and corporate margins simultaneously. The Fed will unlikely be able to provide a rate-cutting tailwind that markets previously relied on. Finally, the labor market, which was already softening before this conflict began, is now doing so with the added headwind of energy-driven inflation. None of that means a market crash is inevitable. However, it does mean that the risk-reward profile of being aggressively long equities at current levels is poor and that patience, at least for now, is its own form of return.

Defense over offense. Trade accordingly.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: AeroVironment: This Defense Stock Had Missed The Middle East Rally
data.description:
Introduction

One of the most interesting topics we can discuss today is warfare and drones, specifically as a modern weapon, for its wide adoption, low prices, and effectiveness. Today’s research will largely be focused on AeroVironment, Inc. (AVAV), as it’s the only drone stock I hold in my portfolio. Still, the discussion will go well beyond this company, as I believe the environment and the U.S. Defense (War) Department’s decisions play a crucial role in the development of defensive companies like AeroVironment.

Probably, it’s not a secret to anyone how difficult it is to fight against drones. Just under a month ago, I was traveling from Indonesia to Israel through Dubai and Amman. When we had just landed in Dubai, all of the passengers were asked to stop in a fully glass-made room due to the drone threat. Needless to say, compared to the discipline and organization of Israelis, in Dubai, there was nothing the authorities could do, no shelters, and no basic understanding of safe positioning during the attack. For me, it’s hard to believe there is a country with no rocket-alert system, and the only notification we’ve gotten of the threat was directly to our phones. The worst part of this whole situation is the certainty that the drone will hit the target, because there is nothing that could stop it. The only question is, what’s the target?

We were incredibly lucky, as the drones didn’t aim at the airport then, and our 8-hour layover had passed with no incidents, besides the fact that we spent over 3 hours locked in the airplane without permission to take off to Amman due to another drone threat. We were literally sitting there and waiting for the drones to finally arrive somewhere, potentially to where we’ve been patiently waiting. Only after we landed in Amman three hours later, did we learn that thirty minutes after take-off, a couple of drones hit the airport, it got evacuated, and it didn’t reopen for almost a week. The closure of the sky was prolonged by another attack a few days after we left.

Answering the question of whether there is really nothing to do about the drone attacks, the answer is mixed. The UAE, for example, has been using Patriots and its F-16E fighter jets. Beyond the cost and ineffectiveness of this operation (MIM-104 Patriot interceptor missile costs around $3 million to $4 million per one), there is a scale problem, which is not solvable by these solutions. Even if the UAE, or any other country in the Middle East, didn’t care about the money and, say, Patriots were free to them in unlimited amounts, they would not be able to intercept hundreds of drones in the relatively small space over Dubai (it’s huge in reality, but not so much for hundreds of drones and as many Patriots). This scenario, by the way, is more than likely when discussing modern wars. Maybe Iran can’t perform an attack of this magnitude operating under constant attacks from the American and Israeli Air Forces, but the experience of the Russian war in Ukraine shows it is more than possible. Even though Ukraine has learned not only to intercept but also to mirror the attacks, it doesn’t mean Russia is not attacking anymore. Just two days ago, they sent over 300 drones, and this is a regularity there, sometimes getting close to a thousand drones per attack.

Imagine if we are now talking about underdeveloped third-world countries, such as Russia and Iran; what could a stronger opponent do? For example, China. Hopefully, we will never witness China in a full-scale war, but in my opinion, we should definitely prepare for this possibility. There is no real statistic on Chinese drone capacity as of 2026. It looks like it’s well over a couple of million, according to the fact that they were over 1 million in 2023, and there is no way of knowing what the manufacturing capabilities are, but it looks like it could be around half a million drones per month. Here, we of course are talking about general drone capability, mostly FPV drones, but the kamikaze part is still enormous. They could potentially be making over 1 million suicide drones per year.

When you think about it this way, I am really questioning the U.S. government's decisions over defense spending, alliances, and contracts with companies. It looks like the government is in no rush whatsoever to work on the matter in question, and this is what is making me question the whole industry there. For example, I’m reading the news about the low-cost drones from the Pentagon, “Lucas,” which are just reverse-engineered versions of the Iranian Shaheds, and I don’t really have the words to describe how I feel about this piece of news. On one hand, it’s great that the government has a solution, which has already proved to be useful in this ongoing war with Iran, but on the other hand, this project has been in development for two years, meaning it’s not even the current administration that put the project together.

The drone, known as the FLM-136 or “Lucas,” was designed by Pentagon engineers using reverse-engineered Iranian technology rather than commercial startup innovation. Officials say it moved from concept to deployment in under two years, a timeline that contrasts with the military’s historically slow procurement cycles. Early battlefield use suggests the drones have played a role in strikes on military infrastructure, including weapons sites and air defenses, contributing to a sharp decline in incoming drone attacks during the initial phase of the conflict, according to defense officials.

Terminated SCAR contract

Before I explain how my government thesis is related, let’s take a look at what happened with the major reason for the AVAV stock drop - the SCAR contract, which was terminated.

One year ago, AeroVironment completed the acquisition of BlueHalo in stock. This deal was valued at approximately $4.1 billion. This acquisition was supposed to bring new segments to AeroVironment and make it a powerful defense giant focused on everything from drones to space and cyber warfare. The main attractiveness of this acquisition was the $1.7 billion SCAR program with the U.S. Space Force in the BlueHalo portfolio, and for this reason, AeroVironment paid a premium on this acquisition. However, the company recently announced that this contract was terminated after difficulties in negotiations. The problem is that the management didn’t do a good job in communicating this to the shareholders. On March 3rd, they issued a “Statement Regarding Active Negotiations for Contract Amendment to Support U.S. Space Force’s SCAR Program,” where they said the negotiations are ongoing, but only one week later, investors could have guessed by the earnings release that the contract had been terminated:

Impairment of goodwill for the third quarter of fiscal 2026 was $151.3 million. In January 2026, a stop-work order was received on the Company’s Other Transaction Agreement for the delivery of BADGER phased array antenna systems to support Space Force’s Satellite Communication Augmentation Resource (“SCAR”) program. We concluded that the stop-work order represented a trigger event that indicated the carrying value of the Space reporting unit exceeded its fair value. As a result, we updated our estimates of the long-term cash flows of the Space reporting unit to reflect the reduced revenue associated with the stop-work order as well as an increase in expected research and development and capital investments to achieve product commercialization, which is expected to result in expanded opportunities and improve long term product margins. The changes in estimates resulted in the recognition of a goodwill impairment charge of $151.3 million in the Space reporting unit.

This is a major hit for AeroVironment, not only for the loss of contract, backlog, value depreciation, etc., but also a strong sign that the management was unprepared and clearly didn’t know how to react to this themselves. Usually, in similar situations, I’d change my rating and exit the position, but in this specific case, I see the government’s side, which I seriously question. AeroVironment wanted to preserve the rights to sell the program's antenna technology to commercial customers besides the Space Force contract, but the Space Force wanted different terms and a fixed-price structure:

We appreciate that the contract was temporarily paused while we work together on a firm-fixed-price contract that provides a commercialized product solution.

I think that the government is playing a dangerous game with its contractors here, and AeroVironment is just one of the companies to be hit. The Space Force started this $1.7 billion SCAR program as a partnership where they’d help cover the costs of building this new antenna tech, but then they tried to change the rules so the company would take all of the costs, and as a result, all of the risks on itself. They wanted a "fixed-price" deal, which effectively means that if there are delays or extra costs, only the company pays the cost. It could be a fair deal if it were agreed upon in the beginning, and also if AVAV could use this tech to make commercial revenue afterwards, but this is not the case.

This is a huge mistake on the government's part because they’re essentially killing the incentive for companies to innovate. Why would a company like AeroVironment want this project if the government is going to block them from making a profit and then force them to take on all the financial risk? For me, while I recognize this as a problem, and will be following up on this story later, I don’t get why the government acts in a way it does while being behind its enemies on drone manufacturing and, most importantly, anti-drone defense.

Despite the loss of this specific $1.7 billion deal, the company still has a total funded backlog of $1.1 billion as of early 2026. If you include "unfunded" or potential future orders, that number is even higher, though roughly $1.4 billion of that potential future backlog was tied directly to the now-canceled SCAR program. The termination was the primary reason management had to lower its full-year revenue guidance to a range of $1.85 billion to $1.95 billion. While the company is currently working to redesign its proposal and win back the contract under the government’s new requirements, the damage that was done to the stock is impossible to take back.

Current and Future Contracts

The company’s third-quarter earnings show contracts across different military branches and tech initiatives, way beyond drone manufacturing, which is great. The most significant win is a $186 million order for Switchblade loitering munitions for the U.S. Army, confirming the huge demand for "lethal unmanned systems" in modern warfare. Beyond hardware, they also have software and high-tech solutions, including a $97 million contract for a generative missile simulator environment and a $75 million project for next-generation biotechnology for the Air Force. Even though the size is nowhere near the lost SCAR contract, it’s clear the company has many important developments for modernizing the U.S. defense in various ways.

Adding to this momentum, AeroVironment was recently selected for a U.S. Navy ISR services contract. As a result, AeroVironment will compete for delivery orders to provide turnkey surveillance services using its JUMP 20-X vertical take-off and landing platform. This aircraft doesn't require a runway or heavy launch equipment, so it will be a perfect solution for the small decks of Navy ships. It has an endurance of over 13 hours and a range of 115 miles.

Finally, there’s a new "Lumberjack" drone in partnership with Northrop Grumman (NOC) and Palantir (PLTR). During a recent Army exercise, the Lumberjack used Palantir’s software to find and hit targets totally on its own, and while I am skeptical of the AI’s ability to decide on targets by itself, mixed with a human operator, this could prove to be great tech to work on. Besides, I am bullish on both NOC and PLTR long-term, and I think AVAV’s partnership with them only confirms the tech advantage this company is able to provide.

Counter drone

AeroVironment is putting a lot of work into laser systems for counter-drone tech, and we know the demand is huge, but there are other companies providing similar or even better value. I still don’t fully get the U.S. government’s position, though it might actually be helping AeroVironment in this specific case. Oddly, the U.S. isn’t partnering more with Ukraine on counter-drone tech, because nobody is doing it better than Ukraine right now. They have FPV interceptors like Spys-A, Sting, and Hunter. Plus, their electronic warfare systems are great at taking down enemy drones.

The whole situation between Ukraine, Israel, Iran, Russia, and the U.S. feels off. It’s obvious who is on which side - we’ve seen proof that Russian missiles are being used by Iran against Israel, showing that Russia is a main partner in that whole scheme. Yet, the U.S. and Israeli governments don’t really talk about it or denounce Russia openly. Living here in Israel, I hear the gossip that secret aid and weaponry are being sent to Ukraine, but it’s not an open partnership, which is incredibly weird. I won’t speculate further, but I believe there is a serious reason for this that no one wants to discuss.

Now, Ukraine is coming to the Middle East to promote its interceptor solutions. They’re making progress and signing contracts with several countries, like Saudi Arabia. The problem is they’ve decided these deals should be government-based rather than company-based, and that’s a major red flag because it means big delays and bureaucracy, which can slow this whole process. This might be a win for American companies like AeroVironment, because with cheap Ukrainian interceptors (they get as cheap as $1,000 per drone), the demand for laser systems most likely will drop.

But anyway, in the end, everyone could have been a winner if they had teamed up. Ukrainian companies and U.S. manufacturers could share knowledge and create something amazing. I think it’s a huge failure of the current administrations for not using the fact that all these countries share the same enemy and have so much knowledge to offer. They’re skipping out on a massive resource. The good news is that the current government won't stay forever in the U.S. (hopefully, it never will), so we can hope the next administration revises the whole defense situation.

Conclusion

As for my position, I originally opened a very small one at around $350 when I first wrote about the company, mostly just to make sure I didn't miss the boat. Since then, I’ve averaged down significantly to about $250-$260 per share. I actually sold most of it again when it was over $350, leaving only that small part in my portfolio. Now, with the recent drop, I’m adding to my position again because I’m confident the demand is still there, and I think the investor interest will spike again.

Interestingly, AeroVironment was one of the few defense stocks that didn't really rally on the Middle East war news, which makes me think it still has plenty of room for a move up. That said, it’s a volatile name, so I’m strictly trading it rather than holding it for the long term. For a long-term hold, I’d personally stick to the larger defense names with drone exposure or a diversified aerospace ETF (for example, iShares US Aerospace & Defense ETF (ITA), State Street SPDR S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF (XAR), or First Trust Indxx Aerospace & Defense ETF (MISL)) rather than buying one mid-cap stock.

Still, it’s been a great trade so far, and I’m rating the stock as a buy once again. In my previous articles, I warned that a major drop was possible, but now, I think the worst is behind us. At this price, the upside looks much bigger than the downside. Even with the unfortunate SCAR situation and the potential for a few more "aftershocks" in the news, I’m buying this dip as a speculative trade.

data.title: TAV Airports: Panic Selling Creates A Strong Buy
data.description:
I covered TAV Airports Holding or TAV Havalimanlari Holding A.S. (TAVHY, TAVHF) in August 2025 with a buy rating as I believed that sentiment pressures due to unrest in the Middle East created a buy opportunity. Since then, the stock has risen 13.7% compared to the S&P 500’s 1.6% return. So, I am happy with the overall return. However, we note that the stock peaked at $33.46 after which the stock price turned sharply lower driven by the company’s full year earnings and outlook and after that another leg down driven by the war in Iran. As a Turkish airport operator, turmoil in the Middle East can have an impact on the stock price of the company. In this report, I discuss, the company’s earnings, the outlook for 2026 and, update my price target and explain why I upgrade the stock to strong buy.

TAV Had A Good Year But Second Half Of The Year Was Slower

TAV Airports reported revenues of €1.82 billion indicating 10% growth year-on-year. However, we also note that the 10% growth figure shows a deceleration in growth as revenue growth in the first half of the year was 12%. Passenger numbers rose 6% with 6% growth in international tariff and 8% growth in domestic traffic. Duty free spendings per passenger declined 6% driven by the fact that company is still ramping up its commercial offerings in Almaty and Antalya. On an adjusted basis, the spend per passenger was up 14% which is solid. EBITDA grew 14% to €560.2 million indicating a 1.2 point margin expansion to 30.7% with free cash flow surging 44% to €222.6 million.

Equity accounted investment showed a swing from a €59.4 million profit to a €59.9 million loss. This was driven by amortization of the purchase price for the concession in Antalya, higher deferred taxes and higher depreciation and amortization and at currency fluctuations.

Overall, the company ended the year with revenues at the higher end of the €1.75-€1.85 billion range. Passenger numbers of 113 million were somewhat soft, as it was below the midpoint of the 110-120 million guided as international traffic of 75 million passengers met the lower end of the guidance. EBITDA of €560 million beat the €520-€590 million guidance while capital expenditures were at the low end of the guided range primarily driven by timing of investments and postponement of some investments.

Growth Guidance Is Not Inspiring

The guidance for 2026 is not an inspiring one. The company guides €1.88-€1.89 billion in sales which points at 3.4% growth in revenues. At the mid-point passenger growth is set at 4.4%. We note that it is not the case that revenue per passenger is going down as the passenger traffic guidance includes equity counted investments and joint ventures, but revenue, EBITDA and CapEx do not include any contributions from joint ventures and equity accounted investments. EBITDA is guided at €590-€650 million marking 10.8% growth. So, EBITDA margins are set to continue improving which is good to see. However, the top line growth at just 3.4% down from 10% in 2025 is somewhat underwhelming.

To that, we can also add the pressure due to the war in Iran. Previously, I pointed the geopolitical tension in the Middle East as a risk but also an opportunity if tensions were to ease. That still holds. However, what we are seeing now is significant escalation of the conflict where high oil prices may reduce flying, and passenger flows potentially offsetting positives from tourism and hub traffic rerouting from the Middle East to Turkey.

TAV Airport Is Now Substantially Undervalued

While the guidance is not one that inspires me and there is a macro risk, it does seem that the stock price has reacted rather heavily on the turmoil while TAV Airports is only partially exposed to volume fluctuations. TAV operates a concession-based revenue model meaning that revenue streams are not purely volume driven and often include fixed or minimum payments. Current estimates on GDP impacts are a 1.3 point impact if the war ends in 2-4 weeks. For TAV, I have adjusted estimates by 0.3-0.8 percent with the impact being front-loaded. Similarly, EBITDA estimates have been reduced by 0.7-1.5 percent and 1.5-3 percent for free cash flow.

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Through 2028, I still expect 6.8% growth in sales and 8.3% growth in EBITDA with free cash flow growing along the same lines. At current prices, we note that the company’s 2025 earnings are not even priced in while EBITDA growth is still expected to be substantial. As a result, I am upgrading the stock to strong buy with a $39.10 base target implying 35% upside. If we also include expected earnings for 2026, the upside increases to 65% with a $47.57 price target.

We see that margins are still expected to improve in 2026, but decline modest in the two years after. Free cash flow in 2026 was already set to be lower due to higher capital expenditures but in the years after capital intensity is set to come down. The net debt leverage is also set to improve even with the company paying dividends again.

Conclusion: Middle East Turmoil Offers Opportunity

There could definitely be an impact on TAV Airports due to the war. However, the company’s concession-based revenue model also somewhat shields it from significant downward pressures. Combining that with the sharp decline in the share price, I believe that TAV Airports could offer a strong buy opportunity even with the company’s pre-war guidance not being an impressive one.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Exxon Mobil: Buy, But Only If You Accept The Oil Shock Premium
data.description:
Exxon Mobil has gained 42.4% since my last report in which I rated the stock a buy. Obviously, the buy rating has worked out extremely well, but the reality is also that I did not have a war with Iran on my bingo card for 2026. While the share price movement following the war is not completely unexpected, we also have to assess whether the stock price is in any way or form supported by fundamentals. In this report, I discuss why I believe Exxon Mobil remain a buy but only when a premium to the valuation multiples is applied.

Escalation Is Closer Than De-Escalation And That Is Good For Oil In The Short Term

The situation in the Middle East has sent oil prices up 70%. President Trump and other members of the administration have emphasized that the war could be concluded relatively soon. That eased the concerns of investors.

However, after President Trump played with the idea of bombing oil facilities during an address, the market again became more downbeat, and oil prices went up again:

We could hit it and it would be gone… and there’s not a thing they could do about it

To me it seems to be more of warning to Iran to open the Strait of Hormuz with no direct intention of hitting energy and oil facilities. However, Iran has not budged while the extended deadline ending 6th of April for the pause on hitting energy facilities is closing in. That has the market nervous as it could force the US to start bombing oil facilities which ultimately could send oil prices structurally higher to a point where a prolonged economic turndown becomes inevitable.

That concern is now much bigger than the concern of a ground war, which would indicate a costly prolonged war. An LHA (Landing Helicopter Assault) ship has arrived in the region, which can be used for a ground war and we have seen some increased movement of military ground equipment. So, right now two escalating cards seem to be on the table namely energy infrastructure bombing and a ground war.

The short-term effect for Exxon Mobil is a very positive one. The Strait of Hormuz typically sees 20% of the global oil output pass through its waters. Typically, a 1% reduction in supply drives a 3%-5% increase in oil prices. So, for crude oil that would indicate a $107-$134 price range and $117-$146 for Brent oil. The current crude oil price is $111 and $109 for Brent oil. So, we are very much at the lower end of the range and oil prices could head higher.

The flipside of the coin, however, is that as oil prices head higher the pressure on GDP growth becomes higher and that may result in the supply shock resulting in economic cooldown and a subsequent demand shock. So, with every step taken away from de-escalation there is a stronger near-term gain for upstream companies such as Exxon Mobil, but a growing longer-term pain.

Revenues And Earnings Expected To Remain Elevated Into 2027

While my concern mostly is about the longer term, Wall Street analyst revisions for Exxon Mobil are largely positive with a 10% upward revision for this year on revenues and 32% on earnings per share followed by a 7% upward revision coupled with a 13% increase in EPS next year. By 2028, we see that revenue estimates have moderated somewhat but that points at the expectation that pricing will offset any decline in demand. So, for now the revision trend is extremely positive but that does not rule out that ultimately we may see rapid economic cooling effects on the estimates.

Exxon Mobil Has Upside On A Premium Valuation

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and adjust the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Exxon Mobil, I am updating my price targe to $178.53. This is not solely driven by higher EBITDA and free cash flow estimates, which increased by 9% and 5.6% for this year but more so by applying a 20% premium against the median EV/EBITDA. This also highlights one inherit risk and that is if oil prices do come down, not only will there be revisions to the financial expectations, but the premium valuation will also fade. So, Exxon Mobil is a buy but with some risks.

We see that margins are expected to increase significantly in the years ahead with the biggest margin jump in 2028 when revenues are expected to normalize but EBITDA still will hold relatively well. Capital intensity is st to improve from 2025 which was definitely a challenging year for Exxon. The net debt leverage remains substantially below the target range of 1x-2x, which is a good thing. My expectation is that the company will continue repurchasing shares, which also enables the company to continue raising its dividend per share without making total dividend payments unsustainable.

Conclusion: Exxon Mobil Has Appeal If You Believe The Premium Is Justified

The right time to buy Exxon was obviously before the war started. If you buy Exxon shares now, that should mostly be because you believe that a premium valuation is justified. I have applied a 20% premium to the median EV/EBITDA and that yields 11%-25% upside and results in a buy rating but with the risk of oil prices falling as they remain a function of the developments in the Middle East and when oil prices do decline that could also result in a normalization of the trading multiple. So, I believe the stock is a buy but investors also should be aware of a potential deflation of the premium.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 266: $26,600 Allocated, $2,914 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
My apologies, this is the first time in more than 5-years that a weekly article in this series was missed. I was in California on business and planned on writing the article on Sunday but had to change my flight and everything got out of whack. This will be a double article in some respects as I will discuss what has happened over the past 2-weeks. The geopolitical conflict in Iran remains unresolved with ongoing military action taking place. Both WTI and Brent have crossed over the $100 level per barrel and everyone is speculating on when energy prices will subside. The S&P 500 looks to have bounced off their short-term lows but its still down -3.84% over the past month. Right now we in an interesting situation as the market is holding up relatively well compared to what the price action in many technology companies is telling us. We have earnings season around the corner, a Fed meeting and an incredibly important CPI print. I am watching everything very closely as I believe the consensus CPI number for April is going to tell us a lot about where the economy is headed. At the end of the day we are buying and selling shares of companies not the economy but if inflation looks like it's going to steadily increase into the 3-4% range it could impact any hope of rate cuts in 2026. I am not ready to make any predictions for the remainder of the year just yet but I am excited to buy high quality assets as the markets decline.

After allocating $26,600 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 266 at $36,046.10. The account's profitability increased (2.41%) to $9,446.10, which is a return on invested capital of 35.51%. Over the past two weeks I have added to the NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI), NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF (QQQI), and Starwood Property Trust (STWD). In week 266 the accounted generated $100.47 in dividend income. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $35.26 (1.22%) to $2,913.80. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $6,332.51 of income since its inception, which is 24.08% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. The reality of the current situation is that nobody knows when the conflict overseas will be finished or if oil prices will surge to $150 before the go below $75. I believe that the market could go lower before the rebound starts and the next several weeks heading into earnings could be choppy. I am looking forward to buying high quality assets that look undervalued from a long-term perspective and compounding all the dividends that are generated. The next several weeks will be interesting and I am especially interested in the forward CPI projections as this will have a tremendous impact on the macroeconomic narrative.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

It's been a difficult beginning to a year that was setup for success. Corporate earnings were strong, inflation was falling, oil was low, and the debate was whether we would get 2 or 3 rate cuts once a new Fed Chair took over. Since the end of February stability has been questionable and while many technology investors are feeling the pain anyone who is primarily in an S&P 500 index fund isn't doing nearly as bad. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's profitability peaked in week 260 at $10,318.48 and since then it's fallen -4.02% to $9,446.10. This is a testament to being diversified and plowing the income back into the portfolio. I am extremely happy with the results because in an uncertain market the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is performing extremely well.

My overall goals were to document how I would build an income producing portfolio from the ground up. I started with $100 and have now allocated $26,600. The rules that I have put in place are that no position can exceed 5% and no sector can exceed 20% of the portfolio. I am focused on income and risk mitigation with capital appreciation being a secondary goal. The chart below represents how the portfolio has done over time and I am extremely happy with the results. I plan on building this portfolio out for many years to come and eventually it will be generating more income than capital I am deploying on an annualized basis.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $981.87 (33.70%)
  • Equities $643.45 (22.08%)
  • CEFs $485.47 (16.66%)
  • REITs $510.54 (17.52%)
  • BDCs $282.46 (10.04%)

Investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself for 30 years in the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year, as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. In 2025, I collected $2,411.27 in dividend income from 831 dividends. After 12 weeks in 2026, $785.60 of dividend income was produced, which is 32.58% of the total income from 2025. There have been 230 dividends generated in 2026, which is 27.68% of the dividends generated in 2025.

There was $100.47 of dividend income generated this week, which brought the average amount of dividend income I have generated on a weekly basis to $56.11. This is an increase of $9.74 (21.01%) YoY. When I look at the beginning of 2026 compared to 2025, I believe that the income path is going to intensify by a large amount. Over the next several years, I should be able to exceed $5,000 in forward dividend income in this account and basically double my annualized allocation to the portfolio.

March ended up being a great month as there was $230.17 of income generated. This was a 21.62% increase YoY. I had previously indicated that I felt I had a chance of exceeding $225 and it happened. When I look at the historical income generated on a monthly basis I am getting very excited about the back half of 2026. I am now thinking that there is a real chance I will exceed $275 in monthly income and potentially break $300 in December. December is traditionally a strong month because many of the CEFs I am in produce capital gains. The chart below indicates that the monthly income is trending in the right direction and I'm excited to see where it goes from here.

The market has declined so much that there are now 43 positions in the table below that are generating at least 1 share per year from their dividends being reinvested. There's $375.86 of forward dividend income being generated from the new dividends being produced from all the positions generating at least one share from their dividends annually. Many positions are still lagging, but I'm making a strong effort to allocate capital toward them so I can get at least 50% of the portfolio to accomplish this goal by the end of the year. My fixation on this is because it will allow me to advance the compounding impacts from the dividend snowball, and it also makes sure that I am not investing in the same positions each week.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

ETFs are exceeding 20% of the portfolio again, and it's entirely my fault. I was also adding REITs heading into the rate cut, which caused the portfolio to get a bit top-heavy. It's difficult for me to stay away from these sectors, especially after big tech crushed earnings. I'm going to work on evening out the sectors and having them more proportional in the future. Individual equities now represent 41.71% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio while generating 22.08% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 58.29% of the portfolio and generate 77.92% of the forward income. I'm working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have sectors close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5-10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

Altria Group (MO) which I have not allocated capital to in more than 2-years is finally under 4.19% of the portfolio. Seeing the way the chart looks is making me smile because the top end is really boarding out. I have tried to work on evening this out but recently I have been taking the opportunity to add to STWD. I am not setting any goals or timeframes on this aspect of the portfolio but I believe that the top-20 positions will even out quite a bit over the next several months. I am getting to the point where I would be comfortable adding to any of these positions as many of them are significantly under the 5% threshold I have applied in my mind.

The top-10 positions have changed as AT&T (T) is no longer part of the group as it has been replaced by Pfizer (PFE). My allocation to the top 10 positions is $7,449.31, and they finished the week with a value of $10,400.34. This is an ROI of $2,951.62 (39.62%). There has been $1,497.34 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 20.10% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $798 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 10.71%. These positions represent 28.85% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 27.39% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 265 & 266 Additions

Ok, I need to make up for missing the article series last week. In week 265 I added NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI), NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF (QQQI). This week which was week 266 I added Starwood Property Trust (STWD).

NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI) & NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF (QQQI)

  • Two weeks ago the market had a horrible week as technology led the market significantly lower. I decided to capitalize on the opportunity and add to SPYI and QQQI
  • SPYI and QQQI are two of my favorite income producing ETFs as they both write covered calls on their respected indexes then retain a portion of the premium generated to purchase out of the money calls.
  • This provides a layer of protection that allows them to appreciate if the market rallies unlike traditional covered call ETFs
  • SPYI focuses on the S&P 500 while QQQI focuses on the Nasdaq 100
  • Both SPYI and QQQI are generating double digit yields and I believe they are both undervalued as the Magnificent Seven have been taken to the woodshed lately
  • I believe that the double digit yields will allow these investments to flourish and I think I am getting great value for the income at these levels

Starwood Property Trust (STWD)

  • I just wrote an article on STWD discussing my full investment thesis (can be read here)
  • STWD is trading at more than a 10% discount to undepreciated book value, with a dividend yield exceeding 11%
  • I believe that 2025 was a transition year as I am expecting that 2026 will have different results as cash has been redeployed and their net lease acquisitions become accretive
  • When I look at the chart and think about the macro I think STWD is a great candidate for a rebound and I am locking in the 11% yield

Week 267 Gameplan

This week will depend on what happens in the market. I am leaning towards BDCs and BDCs that have been hit hard but I want to see what happens with the power plants in Iran before making decisions.

Conclusion

The markets have been fluctuating as there is a tremendous amount of uncertainty. Some are wondering if oil will go to $150 and others are trying to handicap if we will see a rate cut in 2026. The reality is that this is a much different market then the beginning of 2026 and the Iran conflict is turning out to be the black swan event nobody saw coming. The one thing to keep in mind is that April just started and there is a lot of time left on the clock. I have a long-term mindset and I look at these situations as opportunities to add high quality assets to the portfolio. I am excited to continue allocating capital throughout this environment. I am still bullish on the markets for 2026 and think that we will have a strong rebound but the next several months could be choppy and I am focused on inflation, the labor market, and oil. I hope everyone had a great holiday and I am looking forward to seeing how the next several weeks unfold.
